retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Other therapeutic strategies include immune checkpoint inhibitors, cancer vaccines, oncolytic viruses, and chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy. [17] [18] Active immunotherapy might benefit survival in GBM. [42] Molecular targeted therapies are comprised of the following: Mechanistic categories include kinase phosphorylation, cell cycle-related mechanisms, microenvironmental targets, and immunological targets. Key molecular targets include epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and mitogen-activated protein kinase (MEK). [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Aliskiren (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: O'Rawe et al conducted a phase I clinical trial to determine the safety and tolerability of several drugs that target the renin-angiotensin system in patients with glioblastoma. [12] The glioblastoma stem cells contain elements of the renin-angiotensin system. Thus, inhibiting the renin-angiotensin system in glioblastoma stem cells might have anticancer effects. Patients who relapsed from standard treatment, eg, surgery, radiation, and temozolomide, and met inclusion criteria received oral doses of aliskiren, aspirin, celecoxib, cilazapril, curcumin/piperine, metformin, and propranolol. Ten patients out of 17 completed the pharmacotherapy regimen. Two patients reported mild adverse effects. The median survival was 19.9 months compared with 14.6 months for standard therapy. O'Rawe et al state that this difference is not statistically significant. However, the trend of greater survival time for relapsed patients receiving drugs that target the renin-angiotensin system is a promising lead.

retrieved_docs.title: Bevacizumab -- Indications -- FDA-approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Glioblastoma: Bevacizumab has approval as a single agent in patients with progressive glioblastoma following previous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer vaccines utilizing tumor and dendritic cells include a major proportion (51%) of the therapeutic strategies. Currently, 51% of vaccines are at phase 1 trials (phase 3 trials only 7%). [18] They are predominantly targeted for recurrent GBM (55%).

retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the aggressive nature of the tumor, palliative care should be initiated as soon as the diagnosis is made. It is essential to determine the goals of care and patients' wishes throughout the continuum of care. Patients and family members must discuss prognosis to set expectations about the disease. The overall goal is to maintain the patient's quality of life as long as possible with adequate management of their symptomatology. Nevertheless, discussions regarding newer therapeutic avenues and clinical trials should be had in eligible patients. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Afatinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Saran F. et al conducted a clinical trial in patients with glioblastoma multiforme to determine the maximum tolerated dose of afatinib in combination with radiotherapy and with or without temozolomide. The maximum tolerated dose of afatinib was 30 mg daily with radiotherapy and temozolomide and 40 mg of afatinib with radiotherapy. [7] Afatinib is being examined for treating HER2-positive gastric cancer. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Variant III of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFRvIII) - glioblastoma

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Targeted therapies such as BRAF/MEK inhibitors may be appropriate in certain gliomas, such as pilocytic astrocytomas, pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA), and ganglioneuromas. Patients that are incompletely resected or have progression with a BRAF V600E activating mutations may be eligible for Dabrafenib/trametinib or vemurafenib/cobimetinib. These drugs arrest cell growth by inhibiting B-Raf (dabrafenib and vemurafenib) and MEK (trametinib and cobimetinib), which are part of the Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK pathway that stimulates cell proliferation. For subependymal giant cell astrocytomas, an mTOR inhibitor such as everolimus may be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Developing comprehensive treatment plans tailored to each patient's unique circumstances is essential. This involves considering factors such as tumor location, extent of resection, molecular characteristics, patient preferences, and comorbidities. Strategic decision-making ensures optimal therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects and maximizing quality of life. Ethical considerations are paramount in GBM care, particularly regarding treatment decisions, end-of-life care, and participation in clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Astrocytoma -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Consider enrolling eligible patients in clinical trials to access cutting-edge therapies and contribute to advancing treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies have now been approved for treating advanced colorectal and head and neck tumors, and EGFR TKIs have been approved for treating advanced non–small cell lung cancer, pancreatic carcinoma, head and neck tumors, and glioblastoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Gliomas -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical trials : Consider enrollment in clinical trials to access novel therapies and contribute to advancing treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment is similar in MGMT-unmethylated glioblastoma patients aged 70 years or younger; however, these patients derive less benefit from TMZ when compared to the methylated group. Standard therapy with TMZ and radiotherapy is recommended if MGMT status is unknown due to the benefit from TMZ with a tolerable adverse effect profile, along with the lack of available alternatives for unmethylated tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Astrocytoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Recommendations for recurrent tumors include reoperation, carmustine wafers, alternate chemotherapeutic regimens, and investigational IDH -targeted approaches. Re-radiation is rarely helpful; an additional 36 Gy in 18 fractions may be given. Bevacizumab, a humanized VEGF monoclonal antibody, has activity in recurrent glioblastoma, potentially increasing progression-free survival (PFS) and reducing peritumoral edema and glucocorticoid use. [12] IDH -targeted approaches, including vorasidenib and other small-molecule inhibitors of mutant IDH1 and 2 (ivosidenib, olutasidenib, safusedinib), are in trials. Vaccine strategies are also being explored. Recurrent glioblastoma treatment decisions must take into consideration factors such as previous therapy, time to relapse, performance status, and quality of life. Whenever possible, patients with recurrent disease should be enrolled in clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Treatment Fields in Oncology -- Indications -- Glioblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Glioblastoma is the most dangerous primary malignant brain tumor and has the worst prognosis. It is also the most common primary brain malignancy with an incidence rate of 3.2 to 4.5 per 100,000 individuals. [8] Despite significant advances in its treatment options, it still has a grim survival rate. Regardless of the treatment, recurrence in glioblastoma remains a major problem. Moreover, an effective treatment strategy for recurrent glioblastoma has not been found yet. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: No oncolytic viruses have progressed to phase 3 clinical trials. There have, however, been no reports of encephalitis or death. Though completed, 3 phase 3 trials of viral gene therapy have not been FDA-approved yet. Recent focus is on the role of suicide genes. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” comprises the kinase‑targeted agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin. Both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated as potential therapies for acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). As members of the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, these drugs share a common mechanism of action that involves inhibition of specific signaling pathways driving malignant cell proliferation, and their use in AML highlights the therapeutic relevance of kinase inhibition in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1290
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and gilteritinib are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group designated “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” indicating that each drug belongs to this specific category of protein kinase–targeting agents. This classification reflects their shared mechanism of action as protein kinase inhibitors within the broader ATC system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: are also linked to the antitumor category; human herpesvirus 1 functions as a vector in oncolytic virotherapy and talimogene laherparepvec, an engineered oncolytic herpesvirus approved for melanoma, is contraindicated in patients with leukemia, underscoring specific safety considerations. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that antitumor agents span traditional cytotoxic drugs, corticosteroids, targeted inhibitors, and emerging viral therapies, reflecting the multifaceted strategies employed in contemporary cancer treatment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and midostaurin are both classified as protein kinase inhibitors within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system under the category “Other protein kinase inhibitors.” This classification indicates that each drug belongs to the same therapeutic group of agents that target protein kinases, reflecting shared mechanisms of action and relevance in the treatment of diseases such as cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 64
retrieved_docs.content: used in oncology (dexamethasone phosphate and the prednisolone esters listed above). Understanding these interconnections—hierarchical classifications within ATC, dual membership of agents such as anagrelide, specific mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications for leukemia, and contraindications for certain oncolytic agents—is essential for clinicians when selecting chemotherapy regimens, anticipating drug‑specific effects, and managing patient care in oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.
